text: **Anna:** Anna, Pete, I have a {assignment|課題/任務|/əˈsaɪnmənt/|This is a new school assignment.} for you.
**Anna:** A show on {single parents|ひとり親|/ˈsɪŋɡl ˈpɛərənts/|Single parents work hard.}
**Anna:** What is it like for a mother or a father?
**Anna:** To {raise|育てる/育む|/reɪz/|Parents raise children with love.} a child by herself or himself.
**Anna:** We can {interview|インタビューする|/ˌɪntərˈvjuː/|Journalists interview important people.} single parents.
**Anna:** They can share their {experiences|経験|/ɪkˈspɪəriənsɪz/|We learn from our experiences.} themselves.
**Anna:** Yes, but you need to {experience|経験する|/ɪkˈspɪəriəns/|Experience is the best teacher.} parenthood yourselves
**Anna:** Ourselves.
**Peter:** Yeah.
**Peter:** How do we do that?
**Peter:** We're not {parents|親|/ˈpɛərənts/|Parents love their children.}
**Anna:** I asked myself the same {question|質問|/ˈkwestʃən/|I have a question for you.}
**Anna:** I said, Katie, how are they going to do that?
**Anna:** Then an {idea|アイデア/考え|/aɪˈdiə/|I have an idea for a project.} came to me.
**Anna:** I will give you the babies.
**Anna:** You may be asking yourself the same thing that I'm asking myself.
**Anna:** What is Miss Weaver talking about?
**Anna:** I am sure we will find out {shortly|まもなく/すぐに|/ˈʃɔːrtli/|The meeting will start shortly.}
**Anna:** This lesson teaches {reflexive pronouns|再帰代名詞|/rɪˈfleksɪv ˈproʊnaʊnz/|Reflexive pronouns refer to the subject.}
**Anna:** Reflexive pronouns refer back to the subject of a sentence or clause.
**Professor Bot:** We use them when the subject
**Anna:** And the object are the same person or thing.
**Anna:** For example, Miss Weaver says, I asked myself the same question
**Anna:** I is the subject, and myself refers back to it.
**Anna:** Here, she would not use the pronoun.
**Anna:** me.
**Anna:** You need to use the reflexive pronoun myself.
**Anna:** Reflexive pronouns are easy to find.
**Professor Bot:** They end in self or selves.
**Anna:** I have a {feeling|感覚/感情|/ˈfiːlɪŋ/|I have a feeling something will happen.} we are going to see a lot of reflexive pronouns.
**Anna:** Listen for {them|それら/それらのもの}.
**Anna:** Here are your {babies|赤ちゃん/乳児}.
**Pete:** Those are {bags|袋/バッグ} of {flour|小麦粉}.
**Anna:** No, Pete.
**Anna:** For the next six days, this is your {baby|赤ちゃん/乳児}.
**Anna:** Here are your {instructions|指示/説明書}.
**Anna:** Do not {leave|離れる/放置する} your babies alone.
**Anna:** A baby cannot {take care of|世話をする} itself.
**Anna:** And you too must do everything by {yourselves|自分たち自身}.
**Professor Bot:** We will meet next Friday.
**Anna:** Oh, and the person who does the best {research|研究} will get an extra day of vacation.
**Anna:** {Help|助ける/手伝う} yourself to a baby.
**Anna:** Pete, look, my baby is {organic|有機の}.
**Anna:** And whole {grain|穀物}.
**Anna:** Your baby is {ordinary|普通の/平凡な}.
**Anna:** Monster!
**Pete:** This is a great idea, Ms.
**Anna:** Weaver.
**Pete:** This is a terrible idea.
**Anna:** Speak for yourself, Pete.
**Anna:** We need to {throw|投げる/向ける} ourselves into the research.
**Anna:** I'm starting right now.
**Pete:** Hey Anna, you forgot your baby.
**Anna:** Come to mama.
**Anna:** Oh
**Anna:** Good girl!
**Professor Bot:** Singular {reflexive|再帰} pronouns end in self.
**Professor Bot:** Plural ones end in selves
**Anna:** Anna says, we need to throw ourselves into the research.
**Anna:** The subject we is plural, so we must use the plural {reflexive|再帰} pronoun ourselves.
**Anna:** This is {hard|難しい/大変な|/hɑːrd/|Math is hard for me.}
**Anna:** I hope {Pete|人名|/piːt/|Pete is my best friend.} is not doing well.
**Anna:** I really {need|必要とする/求める|/niːd/|I need help with this.} that {vacation|休暇/休日|/veɪˈkeɪʃn/|We plan a summer vacation.} day.
**Professor Bot:** We will all see how Pete is doing in the next {episode|エピソード/回|/ˈepɪsoʊd/|This is the first episode.}
**Anna:** We'll also learn when not to use {reflexive|再帰の/自己の|/rɪˈfleksɪv/|Reflexive pronouns describe self.} pronouns.
